content:
First, observe that we want all three residues
   n² mod 7,  n² mod 11,  n² mod 13
to be equal to the same integer r.  In particular, since “mod 7” takes values in {0,…,6}, that common remainder r must lie in {0,1,2,3,4,5,6}.  

Next, for each prime p = 7,11,13 and each r in {0,…,6}, let N_p(r) be the number of solutions to
   s² ≡ r  (mod p)
with s taken modulo p.  Then by the Chinese remainder theorem, the total number of solutions modulo 7·11·13=1001 with n²≡r (mod each of 7,11,13) is
   N_7(r)·N_{11}(r)·N_{13}(r).

Quadratic‐residue counts on each prime give:

 p=7:  QS={0,1,2,4}, so  
   N_7(0)=1,  N_7(1)=2,  N_7(2)=2,  N_7(4)=2,  N_7(other)=0.

 p=11: QS={0,1,3,4,5,9}, so  
   N_11(0)=1,  N_11(1)=2,  N_11(3)=2,  N_11(4)=2,  N_11(5)=2,  N_11(9)=2,  else=0.

 p=13: QS={0,1,3,4,9,10,12}, similarly  N_13(0)=1, N_13(1)=2, N_13(3)=2, N_13(4)=2, …  

We only need r=0,1,2,3,4,5,6.  One checks that the only r in that range which are simultaneously quadratic‐residues mod 7,11,13 are
   r=0,1,4,
and that

   r=0:  N_7·N_11·N_13 = 1·1·1 =1  
   r=1:  2·2·2 =8  
   r=4:  2·2·2 =8  

Hence there are 1+8+8 = 17 solutions n modulo 1001.

We want 1≤n<2018.  Since 2017=2·1001+15, there are two full residue blocks of length 1001 (each contributing 17 solutions), plus the “extra” n≡1,…,15 (mod 1001).  A quick check of n=1,…,15 shows only n=1,2 work there.  Thus the total count is

  2·17 + 2  = 34 + 2  = 36.

Answer:  \boxed{36}
